title: Blending fibres on a drum carder
subtitle: Mixing colours and fibre types on a drum carder to produce a custom batt
excerpt: A drum carder produces a flat batt of blended fibre faster than hand cards. The carding action opens fibre, removes some vegetable matter, and mixes colours and fibre types physically. Drum-carded batts are the standard preparation for woollen-draw spinning and for producing custom colour gradients from single-colour rovings.
sourceNotes: Drum carding is established practice in hand-spinning instruction. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat, lofty mat of carded fibre produced by a drum carder. A batt feeds into woollen-draw spinning and can be torn into strips (rolags) or pulled off in sheets for different preparation methods.
A controlled blend in which two or more colours are layered and partially mixed on the drum carder to produce a gradual colour shift from one end of the batt to the other.

content.text: A drum carder has two rollers: a small licker-in at the top, and a large main drum. Fibre fed across the licker-in transfers to the main drum and opens as it travels. A
is removed from the main drum using the doffing pick supplied with the carder. A
is produced by feeding colours in sequence and passing the batt through the carder twice.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Carding throws fibre dust into the air. Work in a ventilated room. People with asthma should wear a dust mask during extended carding sessions.

items.name: Washed fleece, commercial roving, or two-colour blend
Table clamp (most drum carders clamp to a table edge)

content.text: Pull the roving or locks into small, loose tufts. Lock the drum carder to the table. Feed tufts slowly across the licker-in, working evenly from left to right across its width. Do not overload: a single thin layer per pass is better than a thick mass.
Step 2: Turn the drum and build the batt

After feeding the first layer, turn the drum handle to advance the batt. Feed a second layer of fibre (the same colour or a contrasting colour). Turn the drum again. Three to four layers of fibre build a batt of useful thickness for spinning.

Insert the doffing pick into the gap at the back of the main drum and run it across the full width to cut through the batt. Roll the cut batt off the drum surface by turning the drum handle in reverse. The batt peels off as a single flat sheet.
Step 4: Blend colours using the two-pass method

For a blended colour: load the first colour onto the drum (one or two layers). Remove the batt. Tear it into strips and re-feed it onto the drum, interleaving strips of the second colour between them. Run a complete pass. The result is a batt with both colours mixed physically throughout, rather than layered.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with drum carding.

items.symptom: Fibre wraps around the licker-in and will not transfer to the main drum
items.cause: Feeding too fast or too thickly: fibre is jamming the licker-in.
items.fix: Feed smaller tufts, more slowly. Use the doffing pick to lift any fibre wrapping around the licker-in before it packs tighter.

items.symptom: Batt is uneven with dense patches
items.cause: Fibre was fed only in the centre of the drum width rather than across the full width.
items.fix: Feed each tuft at a different lateral position: left edge, centre, right edge, alternating. The drum blends left-to-right naturally if fibre is spread across its width.
